did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper proposes and implements a Spiking Neural Network (SNN) based on All-Digital Resonate-And-Fire (ADRAF) neurons for skin cancer detection. The SNN was trained using unsupervised on-chip learning on images of melanoma, normal nevi, and pigmented benign keratosis from the ISIC Challenge 2018 dataset to classify these dermatological conditions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a new Spiking Neural Network (SNN) based on All-Digital Resonate-And-Fire (ADRAF) neurons for skin cancer detection. It details the design, implementation on a 28-nm FPGA, unsupervised on-chip training using the ISIC Challenge 2018 dataset, and evaluation of resource utilization and power consumption. Although published in a conference proceedings (2024 International Conference on Advanced Technologies for Communications), it reports new technical results and system implementation rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: A Skin Cancer Detector Using a Spiking Neural Network Based on All-Digital Resonate-And-Fire Neurons
journal: 2024 International Conference on Advanced Technologies for Communications (ATC)
brief_summary: This study proposes a skin cancer detector using a Spiking Neural Network (SNN) based on 288 All-Digital Resonate-And-Fire (ADRAF) neurons to address the high power consumption of traditional Artificial Neural Networks. The system was designed for unsupervised on-chip learning and implemented on a 28-nm FPGA device to evaluate resource utilization and power efficiency. The model was trained and tested using 150 images from the ISIC Challenge 2018 dataset, covering melanoma, normal nevi, and pigmented benign keratosis. Evaluation results indicated that the SNN achieved 20% accuracy for melanoma and 80% for non-melanoma in the lowest distribution area, which is lower than conventional DNNs like VGG19. Despite lower accuracy, the SNN demonstrated significantly reduced power consumption (10 W) and resource usage compared to GPU-based deep learning models. The authors conclude that SNNs are promising for battery-efficient AI chips despite current accuracy limitations.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the three classes used in the ISIC Challenge 2018 dataset for training and testing the SNN. 50 images were used. The paper notes it is the most dangerous form of skin cancer.
One of the three classes used in the ISIC Challenge 2018 dataset for training and testing the SNN. 50 images were used. Referred to as 'benign' in the context of non-melanoma classification.
One of the three classes used in the ISIC Challenge 2018 dataset for training and testing the SNN. 50 images were used.
A composite category used for evaluation metrics, comprising normal nevi and pigmented benign keratosis. The SNN achieved 80% accuracy for this group in the lowest distribution area.
Mentioned in the Introduction as one of the three general types of skin cancer, but not included in the experimental dataset or SNN training.

all_task_types.target: Dermatological lesion classes (melanoma, normal nevi, pigmented benign keratosis) and a binary distinction (melanoma vs. non-melanoma).
all_task_types.details: The paper describes a skin cancer detector that classifies images into discrete diagnostic categories. The model was trained on three classes: melanoma, normal nevi, and pigmented benign keratosis. Evaluation metrics include accuracy for 'melanoma' (20% in the lowest distribution area) and 'non-melanoma' (80% in the lowest distribution area), indicating a classification task rather than regression.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Challenge 2018 dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) Challenge 2018, containing images of skin lesions.

use.training: Used for unsupervised on-chip training of the Spiking Neural Network (SNN). The images were pre-processed (converted to grayscale, thresholded, resized to 12x12) and feature vectors were extracted using Local Binary Pattern (LBP).
use.testing: Used for evaluation of the trained SNN's classification performance via unsupervised clustering.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It compared the proposed SNN's accuracy and resource utilization against conventional DNNs (VGG19, ResNet50, etc.) using descriptive metrics and direct performance comparisons without statistical significance testing.

ISIC Challenge 2018 dataset.description: Dataset containing images of melanoma, normal nevi, and pigmented benign keratosis. 150 images total (50 per class) were used for unsupervised on-chip training and evaluation of the proposed SNN. Conventional DNNs (VGG19, ResNet50, GoogleNet, YOLOv7) were also evaluated on this dataset for comparison.

Proposed SNN (288 ADRAF neurons).notes: The SNN automatically clustered data into three areas. About 50% of samples from each class gathered in the highest area. The middle area contained 32% of melanoma samples and 34% of keratosis samples. The lowest area contained 32% of nevus samples. The authors note these results are lower than conventional DNNs but highlight the power efficiency.

ISIC Challenge 2018 dataset.notes: VGG19 and ResNet50 achieved the highest accuracy among conventional DNNs. GoogleNet had medium accuracy but fastest training speed. YOLO had the lowest accuracy. The proposed SNN had significantly lower accuracy than VGG19 and ResNet50 but consumed 1/5th the power of a GPU card.

architecture_used.main_model: Spiking Neural Network (SNN) based on All-Digital Resonate-And-Fire (ADRAF) neurons
architecture_used.pretraining: Unsupervised on-chip learning using Spike Timing-Dependent Plasticity (STDP)
architecture_used.input_size: 12x12 grayscale images (pre-processed from RGB via Otsu's thresholding); Feature vectors of 16 8-bit values extracted via Local Binary Pattern (LBP)

architecture_details.layer_structure: Many-to-many Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) topology with two layers: a sensory layer and an output layer
architecture_details.sensory_layer: 16 input paths, each connected to a group of 18 ADRAF neurons with predefined eigenperiods
architecture_details.output_layer: Made of shifters and registers as accumulators to turn spikes into static bits; includes Winner-Take-All (WTA) modules using OR gates
architecture_details.post_processing: Accumulator values summed via wiring into groups with 3-bit step shifts; Total register stores final result
architecture_details.implementation: Verilog, synthesized on Cyclone V 5CSEBA6U23I7 (28-nm FPGA device)

classification_or_output_head.output_format: Static bits stored in output registers representing clustered data areas (highest, middle, lowest distribution)
classification_or_output_head.classes: 3 classes (melanoma, normal nevi, pigmented benign keratosis)

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Spiking Neural Network (SNN) based on All-Digital Resonate-And-Fire (ADRAF) neurons
preprocessing_techniques: Conversion of RGB images to grayscale
Application of zero-inverted thresholding based on Otsu’s method
Feature extraction using Local Binary Pattern (LBP) to generate feature vectors of 16 8-bit values
Removal of unwanted objects such as hair and cilium (background removal)
conclusions: The proposed Spiking Neural Network (SNN) based on 288 All-Digital Resonate-And-Fire (ADRAF) neurons enables unsupervised on-chip learning, allowing both training and detection to occur on a single chip without requiring a GPU.

The SNN achieved significantly lower classification accuracy (20% for melanoma and 80% for non-melanoma in the lowest distribution area) compared to conventional Deep Neural Networks (DNNs) like VGG19 (98% accuracy) and ResNet50 (82% accuracy).
The SNN implementation on a 28-nm FPGA demonstrated high power efficiency, consuming only 10 W, which is approximately 1/5th the power consumption of a standard GPU card (133W–161W).

The SNN required significantly fewer hardware resources than conventional DNNs, utilizing 68% of logic modules and 14% of registers on the FPGA, while using 0% of block memory bits, DSP blocks, and multipliers.

The study acknowledges that the current accuracy of the SNN is not as good as examined DNNs and CNN-based SNNs, attributing potential inaccuracies partly to the limited detail in Local Binary Pattern (LBP) features extracted from small 12x12 resized images.

Despite lower accuracy, the authors conclude that SNNs represent a promising direction for developing battery-efficient AI chips for dermatological applications due to their low power consumption and ability to perform on-chip learning.
important_remarks: The study uses an extremely small dataset of only 150 images (50 per class) from the ISIC Challenge 2018, which limits the statistical power and generalizability of the results.

The proposed SNN achieved low classification accuracy (20% for melanoma and 80% for non-melanoma in the lowest distribution area), significantly underperforming compared to conventional DNNs like VGG19 (98% accuracy) and ResNet50 (82% accuracy).

The authors acknowledge that the Local Binary Pattern (LBP) features extracted from the resized 12x12 images were not detailed enough, which likely contributed to the inaccurate detection and low accuracy.

The evaluation relies on unsupervised clustering rather than supervised learning with ground truth labels, making direct performance comparison with supervised models like VGG19 methodologically distinct and potentially unfair.
There is no external validation; the model was tested only on the same dataset it was trained on, raising concerns about overfitting or lack of robustness to unseen data distributions.

The hardware implementation was limited to a single FPGA device (Cyclone V), and power consumption metrics (10 W) were compared against GPU power consumption, which may not reflect typical edge-deployment scenarios where GPUs are not used.
The paper does not report any statistical significance tests (e.g., p-values) to support the comparisons between the SNN and conventional DNNs.

The dataset includes only three classes (melanoma, normal nevi, pigmented benign keratosis), excluding other common skin cancers like Basal Cell Carcinoma and Squamous Cell Carcinoma, limiting clinical relevance for broader skin cancer detection.
